content.problemStatement:
You are given the head of a singly linked list AAA of length NNN. The values in the list are A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ respectively. You are also given a non-negative integer RRR. You need to rotate the list RRR places to the right.

A single rotation to the right is an operation in which the last element of the list is moved to the first place in the list, while all the other elements are moved one place to the right.


Input Format


The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.




The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and RRR.




The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




**Note:**



For Java language, you need to:

Complete the function in the submit solution tab:


`Node rotateRight(Node head, int R){...}
`
 \  



For C++ language, you need to:

Complete the function in the submit solution tab:


`Node* rotateRight(struct Node* head, int R){...}
`
 \  



For Python language, you need to:

Complete the function in the submit solution tab:


`def rotateRight(head, R):
`

Output Format

For each test case, the function you complete should return the head of the list which has been rotated to the right exactly RRR times.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 100000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: the first line contains two integers N and R, where N is the length of the list and R is the number of right rotations (0 ≤ R < N). The second line contains N space‑separated integers representing the list elements.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‑separated integers – the list after rotating it to the right R times.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Each test case rotates the list to the right R times. For the first case R=1 moves the last element 4 to the front, giving 4 1 2 3, and similarly for the others.
First test case has a single element, rotation does nothing. Second test case rotates right by 2, moving the last two elements to the front.
R=6 is equivalent to rotating left by 1, so the list starts from the second element.

solution.approach: The rotation can be performed without actually moving nodes one by one. For each test case we treat the list as an array of values (reading them into a vector). The effective number of rotations is R % N. The new head will be the element at index N‑R. We can output the elements starting from that index, wrapping around to the beginning. This runs in O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space besides the input array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the list. Overall O(total N) ≤ 10^5.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the list values of the current test case.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it)); R = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        if N:
            R %= N
            start = (N - R) % N
            rotated = arr[start:] + arr[:start]
        else:
            rotated = []
        out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, rotated)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
